title: American Communities 1776-2026
description: Swarthmore College Libraries marks "America250" with a special exhibition in McCabe Library. Drawing on art and artifacts from Special Collections, the exhibition explores how family, land, and nation have shaped the idea of “America,” and invites visitors to consider how they see this country and its history today.

title: Summer 2026 Workshop Series: The Honest Scholar: Best Practices in the Ethical Conduct of Research
description:
Tuesdays and Thursdays in Sci 101 from 12-1:30pm. Lunch will be provided.

Students will learn the basic rules for ethical conduct of research, and the three (3) things that constitute research misconduct. Think Plagiarism! We'll consider responsible conduct of research (RCR) principles and how they apply to your scholarly activities, and research of all kinds, whether in the lab, the archive, or the field.

Presented by Jena Fioravanti, Director of the Office of Research Integrity and Engagement.

title: Philadelphia Connection Masonic Temple Tour
description:
Philadelphia Connection Masonic Temple Tour

Saturday, June 13, 2026 at 11:00 AM

Masonic Temple
1 N Broad St
Philadelphia, PA 19107

RSVP here

Join the Philadelphia Connection Committee for an exclusive tour of the historic Masonic Temple, in celebration of the nation's Semiquincentennial. Discover the enduring legacy of Masonic Founders like Washington and Franklin, whose ideals of liberty and equality helped shape our democracy. Reconnect with fellow alumni as you explore this architectural masterpiece in the heart of the city. We look forward to seeing you there!

Reconnect with fellow alumni during this experience, then join us for an optional group lunch after the tour to continue our conversations. Lunch venue details will be shared very soon.

title: June SwatTalk: The Swarthmore Summer Scholars Program with Ben Geller ’01
description:
June SwatTalk: The Swarthmore Summer Scholars Program with Ben Geller ’01

Monday, June 15
8 p.m. ET

RSVP!

Over the past 20 years, Swarthmore has significantly expanded access for first-generation and low-income students; nearly 25% of the student body are now Pell Grant recipients. For the past 11 years, the Swarthmore Summer Scholars Program (S3P) has supported many of these students as they transition to life at Swarthmore.

Since its founding in 2015, S3P has welcomed 16 incoming first-year students each summer to spend a month preparing for their first Swarthmore semester. The Summer Scholars — who are the first in their families to attend college or come from low-income backgrounds and share an interest in STEM fields — are provided a full academic immersion experience. The program includes coursework in math, lab science, and writing; mentorship from faculty, staff, and students; exposure to the many campus resources that are designed to support their academic and personal well-being; and community-building with fellow scholars — which is perhaps most important of all.

Associate Professor of Physics Ben Geller ’01 is the director of the Swarthmore Summer Scholars Program. Please join Ben for a conversation about the program's history, future, and goals.

About SwatTalks
SwatTalks is an Alumni Council initiative that engages the broader Swarthmore community through seminars featuring alumni and faculty members excelling in their fields. Each hour-long online seminar is free and open to Swarthmore alumni, families, faculty, staff, students, and friends. We welcome suggestions for future speakers and topics — please email your ideas to swattalk@swarthmore.edu.

title: Swarthmore Night on Broadway, The Fear of 13
description:
Swarthmore Night on Broadway, The Fear of 13

Post-show Q&A with star Adrien Brody and members of The Fear of 13 creative team

James Earl Jones Theatre
138 W 48th Street
New York

Get My Ticket

The Fear of 13 is the true story of Nick Yarris — played by Adrien Brody — a man who spent 22 years on death row before being exonerated by DNA evidence. 

As an alumni volunteer for Swarthmore, you have access to orchestra tickets before they open to the broader community. Know a fellow alum who’d love to get more involved? Bring them along. This is a great way to introduce them to our volunteer community.

Space for this event is extremely limited, and we expect it to fill up quickly. If you're thinking about joining us, don't wait — reserve your discounted ticket today

title: Swarthmore LGBTQ Alum Book Club
description:
Swarthmore LGBTQ Alum Book Club Presents: Transgender Histories from Late Imperial China with Matthew Sommer ’83

RSVP!

About the Talk
Join Matthew Sommer ’83 and Adrianne Pierce ’84 for a deep dive into Matthew’s groundbreaking book, The Fox Spirit, the Stone Maiden, and Other Transgender Histories from Late Imperial China.

Winner of several awards, including the 2025 John K. Fairbank Prize in East Asian history, and a finalist for the 2025 Lambda Literary Award, this work is an unprecedented study of transgender lives and practices in late imperial China. Matthew considers a range of experiences, illuminating how certain forms of gender transgression were sanctioned in particular social contexts and penalized in others. A remarkable account of China’s transgender histories, Matthew sheds new light on a range of themes in Ming and Qing law, religion, medicine, literature, and culture.

Matthew Sommer ’83 is the Bowman Family Professor of History at Stanford University.

title: Canning & Preservation Basics
description: Discover safe, simple methods for preserving seasonal ingredients

title: SwatWalks: D.C.
description:
SwatWalks: D.C.

Rock Creek Park Nature Center and Planetarium
5200 Glover Rd NW
Washington, DC 20015

Step outside and connect with your local community through SwatWalks, our new alumni-led series of outdoor social strolls! We are kicking off our very first outing in the D.C. area with a private walk in Rock Creek Park, specially curated for the Swarthmore community, led by the Rock Creek Conservancy. Please join host Ethan Landis ’84, fellow alumni, and your guests for a wonderful morning of conversation and fresh air.

This walk is perfect for all ages and abilities. Whether you want some fresh air, lively conversation, or a great reason to get outside, this is your morning. Bring your Swat spirit — let's walk, talk, and enjoy the outdoors together.

RSVP!

Program
8:15 a.m. Meet in front of Rock Creek Park Nature Center
8:30 a.m. Walk departs

We look forward to celebrating the first day of summer with you at Rock Creek Park. Please remember to bring water, a hat, and sunscreen for the walk.

Warmly,
Swarthmore Alumni Engagement

title: Summer 2026 Workshop Series: AI Use for Research
description:
Tuesdays and Thursdays in Sci 101 from 12-1:30pm. Lunch will be provided.

In this workshop students will learn about the benefits and drawbacks of using artificial intelligence as a research tool, including in depth discussion of the ethical considerations. Students will learn how to access our data-protected, licensed AI tools supported by ITS and Swarthmore Libraries.

Presented by Amanda Licastro, Head of Digital Scholarship and Visiting Associate Professor of English.

title: Generative AI Meets Active Learning
description:
Generative AI Meets Active Learning with the FL-Instructional Designer Network





Part of the FL-IDN Professional Development Webinar Series. Register for Generative AI Meets Active Learning with the FL-Instructional Designer Network.

Date & Time


Jun 23, 2026

02:00 PM ET



Description


Generative AI tools such as ChatGPT, Claude, and others are creating new opportunities to enrich active learning environments. In this webinar, we will explore practical ways instructional designers and educators can integrate AI into student-centered learning experiences that foster engagement, critical thinking, and collaboration. Participants will examine strategies for designing AI-supported activities while maintaining the essential human element in teaching and learning. Through real-world examples and thoughtful implementation practices, attendees will gain ideas for enhancing the quality and effectiveness of online learning.


Learning Outcomes


By the end of the session, participants will be able to:


  • Identify active learning principles and explain how they promote student engagement and deeper understanding.
  • Apply generative AI tools to support active learning through activities such as simulations, interactive feedback, and adaptive tutorials.
  • Develop a plan to integrate at least one AI-enhanced active learning strategy into an existing course or program.

Presenter


Gloria Washington, Ed.D.

Instructional Designer

University of South Carolina

title: Summer 2026 Workshop Series: Communications in a Professional Environment
description:
Tuesdays and Thursdays in Sci 101 from 12-1:30pm. Lunch will be provided.

Through this interactive presentation, participants will learn key strategies for professional written and verbal communication, including email etiquette, meeting participation, and clear expectation-setting with supervisors. The session also explores navigating team dynamics, addressing workplace challenges, and engaging in productive feedback conversations. Students will build confidence and comfort in a multitude of scenarios.

Presented by Kelly Dougherty, Assistant Director Employer Relations, Career Services.

title: Collaborative Course Design and Why Student Behavior Still Surprises Us
description:
Lightning Round Presentations with FL-Instructional Designer Network

Register for Lightning Round Presentations with FL-IDN







Date & Time


Jul 7, 2026

02:00 PM ET



Description


Presentation 1: Bear-ing the Load Together: Collaborative Course Design for Impactful Online Learning with Baylor University


Join a dynamic dialogue between learning experience designers and lecturers as they share how collaborative partnerships, shared frameworks, and values-driven processes have led to innovative, high-quality course designs—both for new courses and redesigned ones. Grounded in instructional design principles and adult learning theory, this session will showcase practical examples that reflect a strong commitment to student-centered learning, Universal Design for Learning (UDL), and accessibility. Participants will walk away with research-based strategies and course development practices they can apply or adapt within their own institutions.


Presentation 2: Your Crystal Ball is Broken: Why Student Behavior Still Surprises Us with Indiana University


It’s not unusual to hear student behavior defined by supposed personality traits: lazy versus motivated, high performing versus unprepared. These statements speak to the frustrations of many instructors but fail to provide a workable solution. The implicit conclusion is that it’s just the way students are. But research into neuroscience and human behavior has uncovered a surprising complexity in the way we think and act. Anyone can be lazy in certain circumstances; high-performing students can still have gaps in their understanding; and unmotivated students can demonstrate great focus and enthusiasm in unlikely scenarios.

title: Hatha Yoga with Stacy Bomento
description: This Hatha yoga class will focus on breathing and mindful movement, helping to increase strength and flexibility. Offering modifications for various postures, this class is accessible to all levels of practice. Each practice will include poses, breathing exercises, and a restorative and calming finish, followed by guided meditation. **There will be no class on July 21**
location: Matchbox Wellness Lounge Upper Level, Matchbox Tarble Commons Upper Level

title: American Communities 1776-2026
description: Swarthmore College Libraries marks "America250" with a special exhibition in McCabe Library. Drawing on art and artifacts from Special Collections, the exhibition explores how family, land, and nation have shaped the idea of “America,” and invites visitors to consider how they see this country and its history today.

title: Crum Woods Walk
description:
Join us for a morning walk through the Crum Woods.  We'll start at the bottom of the Scott Amphitheater and walk approximately one mile to the Matchbox.

Please wear sturdy shoes and bring a water bottle.  This is not a fast walk, but we will be on uneven terrain and walking up and down the hills.

No need to sign up.

title: Pilates with Cathy Raymond
description:
Pilates is a progressive system of exercises designed to stretch, strengthen, and balance both mind and body. Benefits include increased core strength, better posture and balance skills, more physical and mental energy, and improved sports performance.

A recording of each class is available on YouTube.

title: Summer 2026 Workshop Series: Balance and Boundaries
description:
Tuesdays and Thursdays in Sci 101 from 12-1:30pm. Lunch will be provided.

Learn ways to set and maintain realistic boundaries to better manage your time and support your personal well-being. In this workshop, we will explore strategies to find balance and thrive in academic and professional settings.

Presented by Dean Liz Derickson, Associate Dean of Academic Success; Melissa Mandos, Director of Student Academic Mentors and Fellowships & Prizes Advisor; and Ashley Netanel, Associate Director of Health Promotion & Wellness Initiatives.

title: Summer 2026 Workshop Series: Navigating Unexpected Challenges
description:
Tuesdays and Thursdays in Sci 101 from 12-1:30pm. Lunch will be provided.

We all know that life doesn’t always follow a script! This workshop will help you develop skills to navigate around obstacles or unexpected challenges. We’ll explore distress tolerance, emotion regulation, and how to ask for help so you can move from overwhelm into action.

Presented by Simone Collins, Director of Counseling & Psychological Services, and Josh Ellow, Associate Director, Wellness Assessment & Compliance & Alcohol & Other Drug Counselor.

title: Peter van de Kamp Observatory Open House - Cancelled 6/9/26
description:
Unfortunately, the open house event scheduled for June 9 has been cancelled due to insufficient staffing. We apologize for this notice and hope for a successful July 14 event.

The Physics and Astronomy department hosts a telescope open house on the second Tuesday of each month at the Peter van de Kamp Observatory on the roof of the Science Center. Directions are available at www.swarthmore.edu/peter-van-de-kamp-observatory/directions.

The open house hours are 8:00 p.m. to 9:00 p.m. during standard time and 9:00 p.m. to 10:00 p.m. during daylight savings time. The SC-1 exterior door under the breezeway will be open 10 minutes before the start time. The rooftop access door will open at the event start time.

If the weather is bad, the open house will be cancelled by 5:00 p.m., and an announcement will be posted on the Physics and Astronomy department website and the College calendar. Check https://www.swarthmore.edu/physics-astronomy on the day of the open house if the weather is questionable

Open to the public.
location: Science Center Peter Van de Kamp Observatory, Science Center Observatory Computer Room

title: Summer 2026 Workshop Series: Telling your story
description:
Tuesdays and Thursdays in Sci 101 from 12-1:30pm. Lunch will be provided.

Now that you’re coming to the end of the summer program, this workshop will help you reflect upon the skills you’ve gained and how far you’ve come. We’ll explore how your experiences shape your self-understanding and how to integrate new skills into your personal narrative(s). Finally, we’ll consider how this reflection enables you to share your story impactfully as you take your next steps.

Presented by Susannah Fishman, Director, Center for Innovation & Leadership, and Rebecca Weintraub-Barth, Director of Student Activities & Leadership.

title: Swarthmore Summer Scholars Program (S3P) Poster Session
description:
The Swarthmore Summer Scholar Program (S3P) invites the campus community to visit our Annual Poster Session on Friday, August 1st from 10am-11:30am in Singer Atrium.

This July, the S3P Scholars have been learning about how cells divide. For their culminating projects, they are designing, conducting, and analyzing their own experiments to examine the effects of chemicals on cell division. Please drop in anytime between 10am and 11:30am to meet this amazing cohort of incoming first-year students and see what they’ve discovered!

We are excited to see you there!

The Annual Swarthmore Summer Scholars Program Poster Session

Celebrating the S3P Class of 2029

Friday, August 1, 10am-11:30am, Singer Atrium

title: CCC Presents The Sky Brooks Quartet
description:
Enjoy an afternoon with The Skyy Brooks Quartet featuring jazz favorites and special arrangements, including "My Funny Valentine," "I Didn't Know What Time It Was," "How High The Moon," "If I Were A Bell," "Blackbird," and "A House Is Not A Home." Come hear Skyy and Chester Children's Chorus Executive and Artistic Director John Alston perform their very favorite songs, and maybe yours too!  

Skyy is a CCC alumna and recent graduate of both Harvard University and Berklee College of Music, where she studied African American Studies and Contemporary Vocal Performance, respectively.

Admission is free. Seating is on a first-come, first-served basis.
location: Lang Music Building Room 214-Presser Room, Lang Music Concert Hall, Lang Music Building Upper Lobby, Lang Music Building Lower Lobby
